title: Repository Pattern
objectives: Understand the Repository pattern via a real-life analogy
See its role as the single source of truth for a data type
Combine remote API + local cache + in-memory cache under one interface
Implement offline-first data flow via a Repository

sections.markdown:
**Think of a Repository as your household's personal chef.**

- You (the ViewModel) don't go to the FARM (the server API) yourself. You don't dig through the FRIDGE (local cache) either. You just tell the chef 'I want a sandwich', and the chef figures out where to get the ingredients.
- The chef's logic: 'Is the fridge stocked? Serve from there — instant. Fridge empty but the farm is a phone call away? Order fresh, cache the leftovers. Farm unreachable (offline)? Serve last week's leftovers with an apology.'
- **You never care whether the sandwich came from fridge, farm, or leftovers.** You just get a sandwich.

**That's a Repository:** the single interface between 'consumers who want data' (ViewModels, Use Cases) and 'the sources of that data' (remote API, local DB, in-memory cache). It hides the complexity — caching policy, retry logic, offline handling, deduplication — behind one clean API.

**Why this matters:** without a Repository, every ViewModel does its own network+cache+error handling. Copy-paste hell. With a Repository, the caching policy lives in ONE place, is tested ONCE, and every ViewModel benefits. It's the single most important architectural pattern in modern Flutter.

**A Repository** is the single source of truth for a data type (User, Order, Product, Message). It combines multiple **data sources** — remote (API), local (DB, DataStore, Hive), in-memory cache — and exposes ONE clean interface (`Future<User>`, `Stream<List<Order>>`) to the layer above. Callers (ViewModels, Use Cases) don't know or care which source served their request. Google's official Flutter architecture guide places the Repository between the ViewModel and DataSources.
## The layered picture

sections.code:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ UI (Widgets)                                            │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ViewModel / Bloc / Notifier                             │
│    - manages UI state                                   │
│    - calls Repository                                   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ REPOSITORY  ◄──  THE ARCHITECTURAL PATTERN              │
│    - single source of truth for a data type             │
│    - decides which DataSource to hit                    │
│    - handles caching, retries, offline fallback         │
├─────────────────┬──────────────────┬───────────────────┤
│ RemoteDataSource│ LocalDataSource  │ MemoryCache        │
│  (Retrofit/Dio) │  (Hive/Isar/SQL) │  (Map)             │
└─────────────────┴──────────────────┴───────────────────┘

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — a Repository with cache-first + network fallback
## Caching policies — pick the one that fits

rows: Try network; on failure, serve cache
Serve cache immediately; refresh in background
Most content — feels instant, stays fresh
Only touch cache; no network at all
Serve cache; kick off refresh; UI subscribes to updates

sections.markdown: ## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: No Repository.** ViewModel calls Retrofit + Hive directly. Every screen reinvents caching. Grows into a mess.
**Mistake 2: Repository leaking DTOs.** `Future<UserDto>` in the interface — the ViewModel now depends on the network shape. Convert to `User` (Entity) at the Repository boundary.
**Mistake 3: One Repository per API endpoint.** Repository per DATA TYPE (User, Order), not per HTTP call. A single Repository often calls many endpoints.

**Mistake 4: No stream / reactive support.** `Future<User>` is one-shot; `Stream<User>` reacts to underlying DB changes. Modern Flutter Repositories should expose reactive reads (via Hive/Isar watchers, Firestore snapshots, or SharedFlow).
**Mistake 5: Repository holds UI state.** `bool loading` doesn't belong here — that's the ViewModel's job. Repository returns data or throws.
## Best practices
**1. One Repository per data type.** UserRepository, OrderRepository, ProductRepository.
**2. Return Entities, not DTOs.** DTOs stay INSIDE the Data layer.
**3. Expose reactive reads via Streams.** UI subscribes; refresh writes to local → stream emits → UI updates. This is offline-first done right.
**4. Own the caching policy.** ViewModel doesn't decide; Repository does.
**5. Testable via fake DataSources.** Inject a fake API + fake local; test cache-hit/miss/error branches.
**6. Repository interface in domain layer, impl in data layer.** Dependency inversion (see Clean Architecture).
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Cache-first Repository.** Build the sample; verify a second call for the same id serves instantly from cache.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Stale-while-revalidate.** Repository returns cache immediately AND kicks off a background refresh. Show a small 'refreshing' indicator.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Stream-based reactive Repository.** DAO returns `Stream<List<User>>`; Repository forwards it; UI auto-updates on any insert.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Offline mutation queue.** User creates an Order while offline; Repository saves locally with 'pending' flag; syncs when online.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Multi-source Repository.** Combines Firebase + REST API + Hive; smart routing based on data type + user login state.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is the Repository pattern and why use it?**

**A:** A Repository is the single source of truth for a data type — it combines multiple data sources (remote API, local DB, in-memory cache) behind ONE clean interface. Callers (ViewModels, Use Cases) call `repo.getById(id)` and never know or care whether the data came from cache, network, or a local file. **Why:** (1) centralizes caching + retry + offline logic in ONE place — no copy-paste across screens. (2) Testable — swap real DataSources with fakes. (3) Reusable — one Repository serves many ViewModels. (4) Isolates ViewModels from the network/DB shape (via DTO → Entity conversion). It's the single most important architectural pattern in modern Flutter.

**Q2: What data sources does a Repository combine?**

**A:** Typically three: **(1) Remote DataSource** — network API, usually Retrofit/Dio/GraphQL. **(2) Local DataSource** — persistent DB, usually Hive/Isar/sqflite/DataStore. **(3) In-memory cache** — a plain `Map<K, V>` for hot data. The Repository decides which to hit based on the caching policy (cache-first, network-first, stale-while-revalidate, etc.). **Rule:** each DataSource is a separate class with a narrow interface — makes testing easy. The Repository orchestrates them; it doesn't do network or DB work itself.

**Q3: What are common caching policies?**

**A:** **(1) Cache-first** — serve cache immediately; refresh in background. Default for most content. **(2) Network-first** — try network first; fall back to cache on failure. Good for time-sensitive data. **(3) Stale-while-revalidate** — return cache, kick off refresh, UI subscribes to updates (via a Stream). Modern offline-first flow. **(4) Cache-only** — no network. Good for reference data (country codes). **(5) Network-only** — always fresh. Good for critical writes (payments). **The Repository owns this decision** — ViewModels shouldn't have to know. Different data types get different policies (User = cache-first; Balance = network-first).

**Q4: How does the Repository fit with Clean Architecture / MVVM?**

**A:** In MVVM: **View → ViewModel → Repository → DataSources**. In Clean Architecture: the Repository INTERFACE lives in the domain layer (inner); the Repository IMPL lives in the data layer (outer). Use Cases (or ViewModels) depend on the interface; production wires the impl at the composition root. **Repository is the boundary** between the domain (business logic) and the data layer (frameworks). It hides Retrofit/Hive/Firebase — Use Cases and ViewModels don't know they exist. This is why Clean Architecture emphasizes it: it's WHERE dependency inversion actually happens in the codebase.

**Q5: How do you handle offline-first data with a Repository?**

**A:** Three-part pattern: **(1) UI observes the LOCAL source via a Stream** — `repo.watchAll()` returns `Stream<List<User>>` backed by a Hive/Isar watcher. **(2) Repository fires a background refresh** — network → convert DTOs → write to local. Local Stream emits new data. **(3) UI sees fresh data automatically.** No network? Stream still emits from local cache — app is fully usable offline. On reconnect, refresh fires; local updates; Stream emits fresh data. **Killer property:** the ViewModel doesn't know or care about online/offline. It just observes a Stream. All the offline logic lives in the Repository. This pattern is what makes Google's Now In Android + Trello + Instagram feel 'always fast'.

**Q6: Should the Repository return DTOs or Entities?**

**A:** **Entities.** DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) match the SHAPE of the network response or DB row — they carry serialization annotations, may have snake_case fields, might include fields the UI never cares about. Entities are the DOMAIN's view of the data — clean, purposeful, framework-free. **The conversion happens INSIDE the Repository** at the DataSource boundary: `Future<User> getById(id) async { final dto = await _remote.fetch(id); return dto.toEntity(); }`. **Why:** if you change the API's field names or add a caching field, only the Repository's mapping changes — Use Cases, ViewModels, and UI stay untouched. Leaking DTOs upward is one of the most common ways layered architecture erodes.

**Q7: How do you test a Repository?**

**A:** Inject fake DataSources. **Fake `UserRemoteDataSource`** returns pre-canned DTOs, throws on demand. **Fake `UserLocalDataSource`** is an in-memory Map. Test scenarios: **(1) Cache-hit** — pre-populate local, call `getById`; assert no network call. **(2) Cache-miss** — empty local, call `getById`; assert network called + cache written. **(3) Network failure** — remote throws; assert Repository serves stale cache or throws appropriately. **(4) Stream emissions** — populate local, subscribe to `watchAll`, assert emitted list matches. All in **pure Dart tests** — no `pumpWidget`, no HTTP mocking servers. Repository tests are the fastest layer to test and give the highest ROI.

## Real-world usage

- **99% of production Flutter apps** — Repository is nearly universal.
- **Google's Now In Android + Flutter architecture guide** — Repository is a core pattern.
- **Offline-first apps (Slack, Notion, Signal)** — Repository is where offline lives.
- **Firebase apps** — often Repository wraps `FirebaseFirestore.instance.collection(...)`.
- **Multi-source apps** — REST + Firebase + Analytics + Cache, all behind one Repository per data type.

## Advanced notes

- **`Result<T, E>` return type** — Repository returns a sealed union rather than throwing; consumers pattern-match.
- **Optimistic mutations** — Repository writes locally first, syncs to network; on network failure, rollback local.
- **Retry with exponential backoff** — inside Repository, using a package like `retry` or manual `for` loop.
- **Query invalidation** — pull-to-refresh calls `repo.invalidate()` → clears cache + refetches.
- **Composite Repositories** — a screen-specific Repository combines two lower-level Repositories (`UserRepository + OrderRepository → DashboardRepository`).
- **`Riverpod StreamProvider` + Repository** — Repository exposes `Stream<T>`, provider watches it, UI observes reactively.
- **Pagination** — Repository exposes `Stream<PagingData<T>>` for Paging 3-style infinite lists.
- **Real-time collaboration** — Repository combines local optimistic writes + Firebase realtime sync.

flashcards.back: Single source of truth for a data type; combines remote + local + cache behind one interface.
Remote (Retrofit/Dio), Local (Hive/Isar/SQL), In-memory (Map). Repository orchestrates them.
Return cached data immediately, kick off background refresh. Feels instant, stays fresh.
Entities (domain), not DTOs (data). Conversion happens INSIDE the Repository.
UI observes local Stream; Repository refreshes via network → writes to local → Stream re-emits.
Inject fake DataSources; test cache-hit/miss/error branches in pure Dart.

Multi-source Repository: Firebase + REST + Hive with policy per method.
